title: GraphRAG vs Vector RAG
concept: The added build complexity and cost of GraphRAG weighed against its strength on relational, multi-entity queries.

exercises.prompt: In the prior lesson, how did we solve the problem of answering broad, thematic questions over a graph?

options.text: It deletes all minor entities to save storage space.
It clusters related entities and pre-summarizes them to answer broad, global queries.
It translates the entire graph into a different programming language.

exercises.explain: Community Summaries pre-summarize clustered groups of related entities in the graph, making it fast and easy to answer broad, thematic queries.

cards.title: Choosing Your Weapon
cards.body: We know how to cluster graph data into community summaries, but is it always worth the effort? Let's compare when to use the lightweight speed of Vector RAG versus the heavy-duty relational power of GraphRAG.

cards.title: Comparing the Architectures
cards.body: While traditional Vector RAG finds isolated text snippets based on similarity, GraphRAG builds a structured knowledge graph first. This allows GraphRAG to answer complex, relational queries that span multiple documents, but it requires much more processing power and cost to build.

cards.title: Sticky Notes vs. Detective Boards
cards.body: Think of Vector RAG as searching a massive pile of loose sticky notes using keyword matching. GraphRAG is like having those same notes organized onto a giant, color-coded detective storyboard connected by yarn!

cards.title: The Relational Query Test
cards.body: If you ask Vector RAG 'How are Company A and Company B connected?', it might fail because no single paragraph mentions both. GraphRAG easily answers this by traversing the chain: Company A -> owns Subsidiary X -> partners with Company B.

cards.title: Is Newer Always Better?
cards.body: Many developers assume GraphRAG completely replaces Vector RAG. In reality, for simple facts or specific quote retrieval, Vector RAG is significantly cheaper, faster, and much simpler to set up.

cards.title: Side-by-Side Blueprint
cards.body: See how the two approaches stack up when processing your raw documents.

left.text: Chunks text -> Embeds -> Stores. Quick, cheap, great for pinpointing local details.

right.text: Extracts entities -> Links relationships -> Groups into communities. Costly, but maps global connections.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following tasks is GraphRAG best suited for compared to standard Vector RAG?

options.text: Finding a single specific date in a user manual
Tracking how multiple suspect accounts are connected across thousands of emails
Summarizing a single, short PDF document
Calculating the average math score from a simple CSV table

exercises.explain: GraphRAG is designed to map and query relationships across different entities and documents, making it ideal for connecting the dots.

exercises.prompt: True or False: GraphRAG has a significantly more complex indexing pipeline than Vector RAG.
exercises.explain: Vector RAG simply converts text chunks into vectors and stores them. GraphRAG requires running LLMs to extract entities, define relationships, and generate community summaries before any search can even happen.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing trade-off of GraphRAG:
exercises.sentence: Although GraphRAG handles complex relational queries beautifully, it comes with a high upfront cost to build.
exercises.explain: Building the knowledge graph in GraphRAG requires calling LLMs to extract entities and relationships, resulting in a much higher upfront cost compared to basic vector indexing.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements about GraphRAG vs Vector RAG is incorrect (likely AI-generated)?

options.text: Vector RAG is typically faster to deploy because it only requires embedding text chunks.
GraphRAG helps you see relationships that cross over multiple document boundaries.
GraphRAG is highly recommended for low-budget projects because it uses fewer LLM API calls during the index phase.

exercises.explain: Option C represents an AI hallucination or mistake: GraphRAG is notoriously expensive to build because it requires scanning all text with LLMs to extract entities and build the graph.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a system to analyze a complex network of thousands of overlapping financial transactions. Why might you choose GraphRAG over Vector RAG, and what is the trade-off?

options.text: GraphRAG is cheaper to build but struggles with simple keyword searches.
GraphRAG excels at multi-entity relational queries but comes with high upfront build costs and pipeline complexity.
Vector RAG has a higher build complexity because it requires clustering entities into communities.
GraphRAG is always faster to run because it bypasses the need for vector embeddings entirely.

exercises.explain: GraphRAG provides rich, interconnected global context by mapping entities and relationships ahead of time, which makes it much better at multi-entity analysis. However, parsing documents into a graph using LLMs is highly compute-intensive and costly.
